title: Cherry Serving Board with Juice Groove
summary: Single-slab cherry board, routed juice groove, no glue-up at all. The best first project in the catalog.
description:
No glue-up, no clamps, no panel to keep flat. One wide cherry board, a routed groove, and a good finish. If you have never made anything, make this — it teaches milling, routing, and sanding without the pressure of a glue-up clock.

Cherry is the right pick: it machines beautifully, it's forgiving under a router, and it darkens to a deep red-brown over a year of sunlight. The board you hand over looks nothing like the board you'll see in five years, and that's the point.

This is a serving/carving board, not a chopping block. Face grain is easy on the eye and harder on knives — use it for bread, cheese, and roasts.

tools.note: The juice groove needs a core box (round-nose) bit. This is the one tool you can't skip.
Clamped down as a router fence. A straight scrap board works.
Only if you shape a handle.
Squaring the ends. A hand saw and a square do it.
To hold the board and the router fence to the bench.

materials.name: Cherry board, 4/4, 10" wide, surfaced
materials.note: ~$9/bd ft. Ask the lumberyard for one clear, flat board — you want no knots and no cup.

cutList.note: One piece. If your board is cupped, take the cup out before you do anything else.

steps.title: Pick a flat board
steps.body:
Standing in the lumber aisle, sight down the board's length and across its width. Reject anything cupped or twisted — you have no glue-up to hide behind here, and a cupped serving board rocks on the table forever.

A clear, flat, 10"-wide cherry board is worth walking to a second yard for.

steps.title: Cut to size and square the ends
steps.body:
Crosscut to 16". Check both ends with a square — if the board rocks or the ends aren't 90°, fix it now.

Sand the faces flat with 100 grit if the mill marks are heavy.

steps.title: Lay out the juice groove
steps.body:
Mark a rectangle 1-1/4" in from every edge. That's the centerline of the groove.

The groove's job is to catch juice off a roast, so it needs to be a closed loop — an unclosed groove just delivers juice onto the counter.

steps.title: Rout the groove
steps.body:
Chuck a 1/2" core box bit. Set the depth to **1/4", no deeper** — go deeper and you're structurally weakening a 13/16" board.

Clamp a straight-edge as a fence, rout one side, reposition, repeat. Four passes and four repositionings.

Go slowly at the corners. Take a light pass first and sneak up on the full depth — cherry burns if you dwell.

steps.title: Round the corners and ease the edges
steps.body:
Trace something round (a paint can lid) at each corner and cut with a jigsaw, or just sand them round. Square corners on a serving board chip and jab.

Break every edge with sandpaper until nothing feels sharp.

steps.title: Sand
steps.body:
100 → 150 → 220. Get inside the groove too — fold sandpaper over a dowel.

Wipe with a damp rag, let dry, and sand once more at 220. That raised grain would otherwise come up on the first wash.

steps.title: Oil and wax
steps.body:
Flood with mineral oil, wait 20 minutes, wipe back. Repeat 3 times. Cherry goes from pale pink to a rich glowing red the moment the oil hits — this is the best part of the build.

Finish with wax paste. Hand wash only. Set it near a window for a few weeks and watch it darken.
